seo.title: Able Assess vs Manual Assessments | Able Care
seo.description: Side-by-side comparison of Able Assess and paper-based falls assessments. Objective, standardized, five minutes. Everything paper cannot do.

blocks.backgroundImageAlt: Hands fitting digital sensor versus manual assessment
blocks.headline: Able Assess vs paper-based falls assessments.
blocks.subtitle: Objective, standardized, five minutes. Everything paper cannot do.

blocks.heading: Head-to-head comparison
blocks.tldr: Paper assessments like Morse and Tinetti take 20+ minutes, are scored subjectively by observation, vary from clinician to clinician and rarely make it into the care plan. Able Assess runs in under five minutes, scores automatically against normative data, delivers the same result regardless of who runs it and routes risk banding straight to the care team.

values: Four objective, sensor-based metrics scored against normative data
Subjective observation scored by individual clinician judgment
One standardized protocol across every site and every staff member
Varies by clinician, branch and time of day
Any staff member, no clinical certification required
Automatic digital capture with longitudinal trending
Paper forms, manual transcription, no trend data
Automated risk banding routed to the care team
Manual handoff, often lost in transit
Objective data trail for HHVBP, VBP and regulatory reporting
Roll out to every branch with minimal training
Requires clinical training at every site

blocks.heading: Why objectivity matters
blocks.pulledStat: Multifactorial falls risk screening outperforms single-test paper approaches in predicting falls in older adults.

citations.title: Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study
Effectiveness of multifactorial interventions in preventing falls among older adults in the community
Falls in older people: assessing risk and prevention

blocks.heading: See the difference in your workflow.
blocks.bodyText: Book a walkthrough with a clinician who has run both approaches.
